text: robotic assisted repair of a large direct inguinal hernia by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a fifty five year old male who presented with a large left groin bulge and on and off pain and discomfort during activity and exercise. This video demonstrates the robotic assisted repair of his large left direct inguinal hernia with mesh. The robotic platform allowed the surgeon to offer the minimally invasive hernia repair to this patient with a very large and challenging hernia.

robotic assisted repair of a large direct inguinal hernia by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a fifty five year old male who presented with a large left groin bulge and on and off pain and discomfort during activity and exercise. This video demonstrates the robotic assisted repair of his large left direct inguinal hernia with mesh. The robotic platform allowed the surgeon to offer the minimally invasive hernia repair to this patient with a very large and challenging hernia.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament, entering the preperitoneal space and creating a preperitoneal flap. Using robotic instruments, the surgeon precisely dissects around the hernia sac, freeing it from adhesions. The hernia sac and preperitoneal fat are reduced, revealing the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament. Dissection continues laterally, identifying a small indirect hernia with preperitoneal fat, which is reduced. The dissection proceeds superiorly and inferiorly to clear the peritoneal flap attachments over the testicular vessels and vas deferens.

A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap. Using robotic instruments, precise dissection is performed around the hernia sac to free it from adhesions. The hernia sac and preperitoneal fat are reduced, revealing the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament. Dissection continues laterally, revealing a small indirect hernia with preperitoneal fat, which is also reduced. The dissection proceeds superiorly and inferiorly to clear the peritoneal flap attachments over the testicular vessels and vas deferens.

The surgeon uses the robotic platform to insert a large mesh foam bar into the preperitoneal space, positioning it over the terminal defects of the direct inguinal hernia. The mesh is sutured to Cooper's ligament and the abdominal wall using interrupted sutures, followed by securing the remaining mesh with T-seal. The peritoneal defect is closed primarily with absorbable sutures in a simple running fashion, incorporating the previously reduced preperitoneal fat into the closure.
The robotic platform allowed the surgeon to perform this operation with precision and with no blood loss.

The robotic platform has allowed the surgeon to offer minimally invasive surgery to every patient, even people with a very large and challenging hernia.
